source: AXIS Foil Riders Facebook Group + AXIS Foils Official Page

riders_group_posts.author: Andrew Clayton
riders_group_posts.text: I kite on surge 740, adv+ ultrashort, p275. It's the most playful setup iv ever tried! Decided to try that setup with my FD assist +. I couldn't flat water start it, needed a little bump. Had great glide and super easy to pump for such a small foil but it was super twitchy especially controlling pitch. Any one tried similar setup, would a bigger stab help? Thanks
riders_group_posts.sentiment: positive with issue (twitchy pitch on FD Assist+)

key_comments.author: Paul Hurley
key_comments.text: I had the same problem with my surge 830 adv+ultra 320ss. Put a 1 deg positive shim on the tail. Settled down the twitchness and had much better pitch control.

key_comments.author: Sean Richardson
key_comments.text: A bigger stab can help. I ride all the Surges with progressive stabs. Was just out on the 780 and 300p… I use the smaller stabs for bigger foils, so 275p on 830 and 890 and 250p on 1010.

riders_group_posts.author: Nick Restivo
riders_group_posts.title: Which wings are easy to pump on SUP?
riders_group_posts.text: I'm 175lbs and currently SUP foil, para wing (+dw), and dockstart on the 1300v1. I also have an Art Pro 1201 and a 999v2… but I cannot consistently pump either of them for the life of me. I hear that the 1201 is supposed to be a pump machine, but as soon as I loose speed it'll drop out hard on me. I also can't flat water popup on it, but the 1300 is starting to feel real big in the surf and slow in the bumps. Is there a larger Surge coming that might fill this gap? Does something like a FB 1350 make more sense even though it has the same area as 1201? Or do I just have a gap in technique that not letting me pump my 1201 efficiently on SUP? Thanks for any thoughts here. Also my tail is a 360/45 and I ride the armstrong dw v1 107L if important
riders_group_posts.discipline: SUP foil / para wing / dockstart / downwind
riders_group_posts.sentiment: frustrated with pumping, asking for guidance

key_comments.author: Mark Shinn
key_comments.text: Hi Nick, Good news and Bad..... the good news.... yes the Fireball 1350 is a very very easy foil to pump. It is amazingly efficient for glide and forward speed. It will air very nicely with your skinny 45. That was the good news. Now the bad.... the 1201 is not the easiest foil to pump but it is a very good pump foil and if you are loosing height on the mast and then dropping out the sky it is unfortunately a technique failure. As a start place In would guess your pump action is to vertical and you are not generating forward motion with the legs..... Will spending money improve things - yes Will spending money fix the under-lying issue - no probably not.

riders_group_posts.author: Mats Nilsson Hahne
riders_group_posts.text: 80 kgs, looking to learn parawing on 90 litre midlength. Surge 1010 or Spitfire 1030/1100? I have the Surge 950, even if it gets up real easy I think it is on the small side for me to learn parawing? Edit: I am a fairly decent wingfoiler (gybes, tacks, jumps) and have been foiling since 2021.

key_comments.author: Mark Shinn
key_comments.text: The spitfire is an awesome wing wing and has been for a few years now but the Surge has the benefit of several years more knowledge and R and D and is one of the new high camber foil designs. The early starting, pump and glide and of course playfulness are a level up from the Spitfire. I would go Surge 100% - and I do, at just under 100kg the 1010 is my goto for parawing sessions.

key_comments.author: Sean Richardson
key_comments.text: 1010 Surge is my light wind weapon for wingfoiling. I'm 85 kg. I can't comment on Parawing specifically, but if your board gets you going easily enough on your 950, then Surge 1010 could be the ideal wing. I had the Spitfire 1030 previously and like it for super light wind but was a bit slow. Surge 1010 gets going as easily or more so than the SF 1030, and glides so much better - much more fun for bumps and waves.

riders_group_posts.author: Jimmy Jam Foils
riders_group_posts.text: Catching waves with lower motor pod in efoil position on axis integrated mast using fusion. Still can pump and stay on the bumps Small 1-2ft shore break type of day.. but nice conditions for filming and had friends wanting to go out. Local wakesurf and first person to have a foil named after them (Austin keen wake model by liquid force for wake in 2017?) also came to rip the shore break (footage at the end). Got tired of waiting for sets so half way through decided to put the efoil mast on to rip around flat water while waiting for waves. Been catching waves and boat wakes with mt lift efoils for over 6 years now and always surprised to hear people think it's not possible to do with motor drag. It's fairly easy with some good coaching and practice.

key_comments.author: Bardy Matias
key_comments.text: Can you feel a lot of drag tho? I've never even considered tryna ride motor off? You think a Stoke Boost would be similar?
